terms.definition: The volume of liquid passing a cross-section of a stream.
Is an abrupt rise in water surface which results from retarding water flowing at lower stage.
A line joining the points of highest elevation of water in a series of vertical open pipes rising from a pipeline to which water flows under pressure.
A branch of hydraulics which deals with the study of forces (including velocity and acceleration) exerted by or upon liquids in motion.
The weight per unit volume of a liquid at a standard temperature and pressure.
A flow in which the velocity of the fluid at a particular fixed point does not change with time or, at any instant, the amount of fluid particles passing through a section is constant.

The elastic tendency of liquids which makes them acquire the least surface area possible. It is responsible, for example, when an object or insect such as mosquitoes that is denser that water is able to float or run along the water surface.
When the path lines of the individual particles of a flowing liquid are irregular curves and continually cross each other and form a complicated network.
A steady flow, in any length or reach of a stream, wherein the average velocity at every crosssection is the same.
A pressure surge or wave or wave caused when a fluid in motion is forced to stop or change direction suddenly (momentum change).

concepts.answer: Does not remain constant at every place
